Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I first noticed the term Monorepo during a technical research: Code Repository and Document Management in AI-Assisted Programming. At the time, I thought: another buzzword? But after digging deeper, I realized it's unfair to call it "new"—the concept of Monorepo is much older than I imagined.
The Origin of the Name
Let's start with the name. Monorepo is a combination of "mono" and "repository," meaning a single code repository. It's straightforward, unlike many technical terms that try to sound fancy. You might think: putting all code in one repo—isn't that the most primitive approach? Indeed, early projects almost all did that. Whether it was CVS, SVN, or early Git, one project per repository was the norm.
What Problem Does It Solve?
So why did multi-repo (Multirepo) become popular later?
As projects grew larger, codebases expanded, builds became slower, and permission control became harder to fine-tune. People started thinking: why not split the big repo into smaller ones? Thus came the wave of microservices and component-based repositories, where each service or module had its own Git repo with independent releases and deployments. This model seemed great—until teams grew to a certain size, and developers started jumping between dozens…
